trajectory_summary: The call opened with a genuinely engaged customer who volunteered clear bill-pain motivation and cooperated fully through a detailed property description. The rep's tool failure at 01:05 was navigable — Yovanni remained helpful and descriptive through 02:33 — but the rep had no verbal pivot protocol and exited the call without completing a single verify item, deepening the bill anchor, or locking a callback time. An engaged lead was suspended, not converted.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the correct classification is 'callback promised, no appointment set, no locked time.' This is a soft callback — not a booked appointment and not a clean DQ. The distinction matters for no-show risk: an unlocked callback on a paid lead is a likely miss.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Locked callback discipline — every exit needs a time
primary_coaching_focus.quote: So let me just talk to them, and then I can give you a callback here a little bit later.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The call ended with zero qualification completed and an unlocked, unscheduled callback. On a paid lead, this is a likely miss. The fix is a single habit: before ending any call without an appointment, lock a specific callback time with the customer. This is the highest-leverage coaching intervention on this call because it applies to every tool-failure scenario Tyke will encounter going forward, not just this one.

key_moments.quote: the mainly just the prices of the electric bill recently as well as it's just easy to provide for myself and not have to worry about things like that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Yovanni handed the rep a two-part motivation — bill pain and a self-sufficiency value — within the first 45 seconds. The rep acknowledged it ('No. You're okay.') and moved immediately to the map tool. The bill anchor was never deepened, and the self-sufficiency angle was never touched again. This is the emotional core of the pitch, and it was captured and dropped.

key_moments.quote: Let me get a better view of it. It's not automatically pinning the home.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep hit a satellite imagery tool failure and had no verbal pivot. The correct move was to acknowledge the tool issue briefly, then continue the verify sequence verbally — utility company, bill amount, roof age, title/ownership — while the design team resolved the imagery. Instead, the rep spent the next 90 seconds collaborating with Yovanni on property identification, which is design-team work, not setter work. No schema section directly covers satellite imagery tool failure during address verification — this is a protocol gap for Kenny to address.

key_moments.quote: It's a single family, trailer home.
key_moments.stewart_read: Yovanni identified the home as a single-family trailer home. The rep did not follow up to confirm ownership — verify.address_homeowner_confirmed requires ownership confirmation, not just home type. Whether manufactured/mobile homes are eligible for Ion's program is not addressed in the schema's dq_rules section and should be treated as a Kenny/TBD item, not a disqualifier. What IS grounded: the rep never asked 'And you own the home?' — a required verify item that was skipped.

key_moments.quote: You'll see about, like, three buildings on that property
key_moments.stewart_read: Yovanni mentioned three buildings on the property. This is a potential secondary-structure signal — detached structures (garages, workshops, barns) can represent additional solar or roofing opportunity. The rep was focused on property identification and did not register this as a cross-sell signal. The signal is soft — Yovanni was describing the property layout, not asking about secondary structures — but it warranted a mental note and potentially a brief probe.
